data.title: Broadcom, CrowdStrike To Report Results As Investors Look To Jobs Report
data.description:
Get ahead of the market by subscribing to Seeking Alpha's Wall Street Week Ahead, a preview of key events scheduled for the coming week. The newsletter keeps you informed of the biggest stories set to make headlines, including upcoming IPOs, investor days, earnings reports, and conference presentations.

Investors will be focused on Friday’s jobs report in the U.S. for February. Purchasing managers data for February will be released on Monday from S&P Global and the Institute for Supply Management, while factory and durable goods order statistics for January will be released on Wednesday. Meanwhile, EU inflation data for last month will be out on Monday, and February retail sales data are due out on Thursday.

CrowdStrike (CRWD), Broadcom (AVGO), Target (TGT), Costco Wholesale (COST), and Kroger (KR) will report earnings next week.

Earnings spotlight: Monday, March 3 - Okta (OKTA), GitLab (GTLB), AST SpaceMobile (ASTS), California Resources (CRC), and Surgery Partners (SGRY). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Tuesday, March 4 - CrowdStrike, Sea Ltd. (SE), Target, AutoZone (AZO), and Ross Stores (ROST). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Wednesday, March 5 - Marvell Technology (MRVL), Veeva Systems (VEEV), Zscaler (ZS), Brown-Forman (BF.A)(BF.B), and Campbell’s (CPB). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Thursday, March 6 - Broadcom (AVGO), Costco Wholesale, Kroger, Burlington Stores (BURL), and BJ’s Wholesale Club Holdings (BJ).

Volatility watch: Intuitive Machines (LUNR) and Capricor Therapeutics (CAPR) have a high level of implied volatility based on options trading. SoundHound AI (SOUN) and Aehr Test Systems (AEHR) head into the new week with a high level of short interest outstanding on them. The most overbought stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include Power Metals Corp (OTCQB:PWRMF), QVC Group (QVCGB), and China Yuchai (CYD). The most oversold stocks per their 14-day Relative Strength Index include Parsons (PSN) and Brazil Potash (GRO).

IPO watch: Kestra Medical Technologies (KMTS) is expected to price its IPO on Tuesday and begin trading on Wednesday. The lockup periods for Pheton Holdings (PTHL) and Powell Max (PMAX) expire on Tuesday.

Dividend watch: Companies that have an ex-dividend next week include McDonald’s (MCD), Nike (NKE), Lockheed Martin (LMT), Sherwin Williams (SHW), and McKesson (MCK). Companies forecast to increase their quarterly dividends include American Express (AXP), from $0.70 to $0.82, Kadant (KAI), from $0.32 to $0.35, and Academy Sports and Outdoors (ASO), from $0.11 to $0.12. Read through some of the dividend stock picks from Seeking Alpha analysts.

Investor events: MWC Barcelona, also known as Mobile World Congress, will take place over four days starting Monday. The annual gathering brings together global leaders in the technology, mobile, and connectivity industries.

Broadcom earnings preview: Broadcom is scheduled to report its latest quarterly results on Thursday. The semiconductor company is expected to post EPS of $1.51 on revenue of $14.59B. Oppenheimer said in a note this week that it seems upside in the most recent quarter and current three-month period, driven by application-specific integrated circuits and networking. The firm said it expects new AI ASIC projects with OpenAI and Apple (AAPL) to contribute next year. “We believe AVGO has one of the most strategically and financially attractive business models in semiconductors,” Oppenheimer said.

CrowdStrike Holdings earnings preview: CrowdStrike is scheduled to report earnings on Tuesday. The cybersecurity firm is expected to post EPS of $0.86 on revenue of $1.03B. Truist said in a note earlier this month that the impact from the global outage last summer seems to be behind the company. “We believe the company has built in enough cushion in its guidance to account for delayed outbound prospecting, which has since fully resumed and is rising to pre-incident levels of responses,” Truist said. The note said CrowdStrike is continuing to benefit from recent “cyberattacks and is one of the best positioned security companies to benefit from vendor consolidation based on product breadth in our view.” Truist reiterated its Buy rating on CrowdStrike and raised its price target to $460 from $385.

data.title: USIG: Inflation Expectations Are Rising, Bearish On Duration Bets
data.description:
The iShares Broad USD Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF (NASDAQ:USIG) is a rather high duration bond ETF, therefore exposed to rate risks quite considerably, particularly longer-term rates that are more volatile in the current environment given questions around the levels of inflation. We continue to worry in the US about the current administration's trade policies, while perhaps effective at tackling geopolitical issues, aggravates the sensitive area of inflation and inflation expectations. In terms of credit spreads, they remain at historical lows. While the economic engine isn't showing any alarming signs in the US, rates are still elevated compared to previous periods, and nominally high rates can still matter for more distressed parts of the economy, so historically low credit spreads compared to periods with much fewer economic issues strikes us as unusual.

USIG Breakdown

Firstly, duration is above 6.5 years, which constitutes the sensitivity factor to step changes in rates. With longer dated securities, particularly rates implied on the further part of the yield curve matter, since those are compounded for more periods and take a bigger toll on bond values through discounting. Most off the bonds are rated between A and BBB rated at almost 90% of USIG's allocations.

The yield curve currently looks like this. Note the significant changes in rates even in long-dated maturities, which means considerable changes in outlook around long-term rate expectations, which will be affected more by structural rather than transitory factors.

Remember, that inflation is both caused by current supply and demand dynamics, but also by expectations about future supply and demand dynamics, or even just expectations about other people's expectations about future supply and demand dynamics. Expectations of inflation create inflation even where there might not be structural scarcity or especially high demand due to inflation expectations affecting wage negotiations, which in turn causes companies to cover higher costs with their own price increases, which is definitionally inflation. Inflation expectations are the primary and most important cause of inflation and are tracked through surveys like those conducted by Michigan University. Inflation expectations can anchor inflation at a higher level, and are therefore that structural factor we mentioned earlier that affects long-term rates. For the last year inflation expectations have averaged around 2.7% or so. In the last three months it's risen a lot, to 3.3% and now the latest surveys showing 4.3% for the 1-year expectation. This is a powerful leading indicator for inflation, and just this fact alone has us eschewing long-duration bonds. Latest CPI data has also been above target once again at 3%, signaling reinflation, with comps also getting harder since the base includes a lower oil price and already deflated commodities after COVID-19.

The reason for the higher inflation expectations comes down to the consuming public's assessment around risks to prices of the goods they purchase due to Trumpflation. Tariffs have been pledged against neighbours, requiring them to come to the table on other issues like Fentanyl and the cartels. They have been threatened against the EU as well. All of this will be rather inflationary given how much the US imports. It has caused stocking activity, which is naturally inflationary and one of the initial inflation impulses in this inflation saga started during COVID-19.

On the other hand, an end to the war in Ukraine could be a bit disinflationary for the US, where traders have been betting on the end to sanctions. Europe may continue with sanctions on Russia, but that's not important for the US rate discussion.

Bottom Line

Mostly we are concerned about inflation expectations which are more important for long-term rates than any other factor, and the survey data shows that inflation is pointing up and not down, hence the Fed's wait and see stance.

The other thing, though less important, not playing in USIG's favour are the credit spreads, which are at historical lows. Credit spreads for BBB rated securities are almost half what they were in very easygoing periods like 2017. We think there are certainly more economic and credit risks in the complicated environment of today compared to that of 2017, though maturity walls have been softened, and the economy has really been resilient.

USIG's expense ratio is negligible at 0.04%, which is very solid among long-duration fixed income ETFs. A good expense ratio would be below 0.15% for long-dated fixed income ETFs, and USIG is way below that. But that's not a reason to pick it when it stands in the way of unpredictable risks around inflation expectations, linked meaningfully to not just economic policy but also Trump's foreign policy strategies where he is threatening other nations with tariffs unless geopolitical concessions are made.

data.title: Corporate U.S. Clean Energy Purchases Near 120 GW, Driven By AI Datacenter Boom
data.description:
The rise of AI has caused expectations for datacenter electricity demand - and by extension, overall power demand - to skyrocket since the unveiling of ChatGPT in the fall of 2022, prompting a rush toward clean energy purchases. Corporations in the US have contracted nearly 48 GW of additional clean energy capacity in the 12 months since our February 2024 update, with Big Tech driving most of the activity.

Key Findings

➤ Overall, tracked clean energy capacity contracted by corporate offtakers in the US jumped 66.4% between our 2024 and 2025 updates.

➤ The technology and web services sector led the surge, contributing nearly 92% of total new additions.

➤ Nuclear energy makes up 43% of the tracked 47.6-GW increase year over year.

➤ Corporate buyers contracted nearly 14 GW of US solar capacity in 2024, compared to about 2.2 GW of wind capacity.

➤ Texas continues to lead all US states in corporate-tied clean energy capacity, accounting for 27.6% of the tracked total, but Missouri, Pennsylvania and North Dakota experienced the largest annual increases.

➤ US offtakers account for more than 92% of tracked clean energy corporate procurement in the US, according to S&P Global Commodity Insights tracking. European corporations make up the bulk of the balance.

Tech companies pivot to nuclear

Faced with unprecedented load growth due to the rising need for energy-intensive AI datacenters, US tech companies dominated the corporate energy procurement market in the interval under review. Recent projections have datacenter energy consumption doubling to nearly 800 TWh by 2030 - more than triple the amount of electricity California consumed in 2023. To maintain pace with ambitious, self-imposed clean energy targets, datacenter companies were forced to take assertive action in energy procurement.

Microsoft Corp. (MSFT) and Brookfield Renewable Corp. (BEPC) announced one of the largest corporate renewable deals in history in May 2024, partnering to add 10.5 GW of renewable capacity to the grid in the US and Europe by 2030. Seven months later, Switch Inc. announced a partnership with advanced nuclear technology startup Oklo Inc. (OKLO) to build 12 GW of nuclear energy. Based on S&P Global Commodity Insights tracking, US tech companies signed clean energy deals across the US and the rest of the world totaling over 47 GW between February 2024 and February 2025.

Switch's announcement with Oklo was not the only nuclear deal announced in 2024. Nuclear generation resurged in 2024, given the technology's ability to service large loads without interruption and without emitting greenhouse gases. Over 20 GW worth of nuclear deals were announced since February 2024 - all to tech companies - though most of this capacity is unlikely to come online before 2030. See S&P Global Commodity Insights' 2025 US datacenters and nuclear energy report.

Solar remains the preferred technology for corporate offtakers securing clean energy capacity in the US, however, accounting for 49.1% of overall corporate-tied US clean-energy capacity tracked as of this report. Wind is a distant second, representing only 23.9% of the total.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Africa Oil Corp.'s Earnings Spell Opportunity
data.description:
Africa Oil Corporation (OTCMKTS: OTCPK:AOIFF) announced its 4Q 2024 earnings. Despite tough oil prices and some production issues in Nigeria, the company managed to more than justify its almost $600 million USD market cap. As we'll see throughout this article, the company will have a more impressive 2025, which will enable strong shareholder returns.

Results

The company managed to achieve respectable results based on its current asset operations.

The company had 17k barrels / day in full-year average daily working interest, slightly in line, but below management guidance. The company recorded 2024 FY CFFO of almost $270 million, just slightly below management guidance. Prime has $175 million in net debt, which will double when the company makes a pro forma merger, but it's more than manageable.

The company had 101% proved reserves with more than $1 billion in 2P NPV reserves. The company has almost 30 million barrels in reserves, and is producing roughly 5 million barrels / day as the asset continues to decline.

Financial Results

The company's financial results show a continued ability to perform.

The company's production declined slightly YoY but EBITDAX for the year went up along with FCF. Earning almost $200 million in attributable FCF, is enough to pay down the company's debt at current prices driving strong returns. The company has more than $60 million in cash, and it managed to pay off a staggering $125 million in debt.

That's with no lifting in Q4 2024 which will help the company in 2025.

BTG Integration

The company is roughly a week from closing its merger with BTG Oil & Gas.

The company will double its immediate FCF with BTG Oil and Gas receiving 35.5% of the outstanding share capital of the outstanding capital. That's enlarged from 35%, due to Africa Oil Corporation's repurchases of its shares. We'd like to see share repurchases continue in the upcoming years, and even ramp up with the company's FCF.

The company is moving to $100 million in annual dividends with a new combined $900 million market cap. The company's pro forma $0.15 annual dividend is a double-digit dividend yield as the base yield that the company can comfortably afford. The company will still have substantial FCF on top of that.

The company earned $68 million to shareholders in 2024, a double-digit return, and it's doubling FCF in 2025 with a much smaller enlarged share count.

Venus Oil

For the long-term the company also has an exciting development opportunity in Venus Oil.

The company has a net 4% interest in Block 2913B through its shareholding in Impact Oil & Gas. The field is expected to see the FID in a year, with 150k barrels / day in production. That's 6k barrels / day in attributable production to the company from the first development. With additional drilling and developments it will be essential for the company's portfolio.

While production is expecting to trend down from 2025-2027, it'll pick up some in 2028, and improve from there. TotalEnergies is covering the development costs, with a fully carry, and the field has billions of barrels of oil. The 4% attributable to Africa Oil Corporation is enough to support 10s of thousands of barrels alone, with Guyana style developments.

The company has even larger stakes in the South Africa Orange Basin, but development is continuing there. The company has the cash flow to run development with long-term potential here.

Future Shareholder Returns

The company has the ability to drive future shareholder returns. The company sits at a $900 million market cap. Simply based on 2024, the company will earn almost $400 million in FCF. Continuing to 2027, even with the planned production decline, the company will still have $250 million in FCF. The company has $350 million in debt through Prime, $275 million counting cash.

The company is committing to $100 million in dividends, which it can comfortably afford, but we'd like to see it match that with share buybacks. Regardless, the company can within a single year, pay its debt down to $0, and pay a double-digit shareholder return. For now, repurchases in 2025 are capped at $14-15 million.

We don't see another company that's as obviously undervalued.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is crude oil prices. The company is profitable at current levels. However, there's real recession risk in the economy, and there's pressure to increase production. That could push down prices, which could substantially hurt Africa Oil Corporation's ability to drive its future shareholder returns.

Conclusion

Africa Oil Corporation is about to wrap off its merger with BTG Group and initiate a double-digit dividend yield. That's a base dividend yield that the company can comfortably afford. The company is continuing to generate massive FCF, enough to comfortably handle its debt, pay dividends, and repurchase shares.

Putting all this, at its current share price, Africa Oil Corporation is a valuable investment. Let us know your thoughts in the comments below.

data.title: Buy The Dip With 10% Yield: BrightSpire Capital
data.description:
Co-authored by Treading Softly

"Life is about timing"

– Carl Lewis

When planning to be defensive or offensive, timing can be massively important. Whenever we observe a sector in the stock market that is enduring a lot of negative sentiment or a sector that is benefiting from a ton of positive sentiment, the timing of management's actions can vastly change the outcome. Acting too soon in a defensive nature can cause you to lag behind your more aggressive peers. However, being aggressive for too long can cause you to have a cataclysmic fall to the other side, meaning that a defensive management team may not post the same stellar results but is less likely to post harmful results over the long run. A muted reaction from the market is usually caused by a conservative management team versus an excited reaction from the market due to those teams who are more aggressive, whether positive or negative.

The commercial mortgage REIT sector has been the source of drama this earnings season. We saw Apollo Commercial Real Estate (ARI) rally double digits as it produced an outlook for growing its portfolio and redeploying capital that was previously tied up in non-performing loans. Then we saw Ares Commercial Real Estate (ACRE) drop double digits as its management issued an outlook that it would take more time to deal with its defaults, and it didn't expect to grow its portfolio anytime soon, necessitating a dividend cut.

Today, I want to dive into the earnings of another commercial mREIT that we've covered before to see where their earnings landed them.

Let's dive in!

A Sector With Wide-Ranging Outcomes

BrightSpire Capital, Inc. (BRSP), yielding 10.3%, is a commercial mREIT with owns and originates a portfolio of loans or mortgages tied to commercial properties. The commercial real estate market has been hit hard by rising interest rates and reduced interest from large companies in owning or leasing properties when they became less economically attractive.

The earnings from BRSP when compared to ARI and ACRE were right in the middle. BRSP management warned us that adjusted distributable earnings would decline, and they did to $0.18/share. However, it appears the dividend was reduced enough and earnings continued to out-earn the dividend. Source

Book value took a large hit in Q2 2024, but has been relatively stable quarter to quarter since then.

Throughout 2024, BRSP's focus has been on maintaining a high level of liquidity while managing the loans on its watchlist. In Q4, we saw some progress with three loans coming off of the watchlist and one loan being added to it.

BRSP provides investors with significant details about the loans on their watchlist.

The largest loan by far is the hotel in San Jose in California. BRSP has filed paperwork to foreclose on the property, and the company that owns it is currently in bankruptcy court. That is good news because the owners have been working to find a new loan to refinance BRSP and a mezzanine loan owned by Hilton. There are news reports that a new lender has been found willing to refinance the entire mortgage. So while the loan is a 5 on their risk scale because it is in active foreclosure, there is a lot of reason to believe that BRSP will experience a full recovery of their capital on this loan.

In the earnings release, management commentary was short and sweet:

"Looking ahead, we expect to continue to resolve watchlist and REO assets and grow the portfolio and earnings through new loan originations."

On the origination front, BRSP originated $53 million in new loans.

That isn't a ton, considering they had $93 million in loans repaying, and in the last earnings call management suggested that they wanted to increase the overall size of the portfolio by approximately $1 billion in 2025. However, this should be viewed in the context that BRSP has not originated a new loan since Q4 2022. That's right, for two years BRSP has not been in the market making loans. It has been coasting on its prior portfolio which was $3.5 billion in loans and that portfolio has slowly shrunk over the past two years.

So coming from a complete dead stop, we can expect it to take time to ramp up their origination pipeline. The company noted in their subsequent events that they have originated another $53 million in loans so far in 2025. That means in Q1 we will see higher originations than in Q4.

BRSP pulled in their horns before everyone else did. Management allowed the portfolio to shrink from over $3.5 billion to about $2.5 billion. Now, they are originating enough so that the portfolio isn't shrinking. In the first half of 2025, we expect that BRSP's portfolio will be growing. Ultimately, that is a positive for distributable earnings and that will lead to the dividend growing again.

We expect that in Q1 management will keep the dividend where it is, and given management's history of being somewhat conservative with their payout ratio, it will likely remain flat for Q2 as well. It will be Q3 or Q4, where the portfolio is growing and BRSP is able to redeploy capital that is extracted from their watchlist loans that a return to dividend growth will be on the table.

This earnings likely won't be seen as a home run by the market. However, it is an inflection point for the company where they have turned from 100% defense to playing some offense. For years, BRSP has been hunkered down, waiting for economic conditions to change, and in Q4 they decided conditions changed enough to resume growth. BRSP is trading at a bargain price and investors should take advantage of it.

Conclusion

BRSP has decided to shift its footing.

BRSP has spent numerous quarters allowing its portfolio to wind down and standing in a purely defensive position. This allowed them to avoid many of the pitfalls their peers have fallen into by being overly aggressive during a period of sector-wide weakness. Now, they're in the process of ramping up their portfolio again. We're seeing a complete change in their focus from being completely defensive to being more aggressive in their lending. This will allow them to see their earnings ramp up. It's hard to generate the same level of earnings from a portfolio that's only $2.5 billion versus a portfolio that's $3.5 billion in value. This ramp-up is a benefit as they'll have the ability to cover their current dividend, as we've seen from these most recent earnings, and will continue to support it as they ramp up.

In retirement, you can unlock higher levels of income by investing a portion of your portfolio in down-trodden sectors. Often, they are undervalued and higher-yielding due to the negative sentiment that swirls around them. BRSP is a great example of a firm that's sold off with peers in sympathy but took a more defensive approach, allowing it to be on solid footing for the sector's recovery.

data.title: Duolingo Q4 Earnings: Profitability Pitstop Has Arrived
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

I last wrote about Duolingo (DUOL) in January, where I rated the stock a “hold” with a price target of $306, which was 13% below the price at publication.

In the post, I admitted that I had been too conservative with my previous price targets on the stock as I underestimated Duolingo’s capability to expand its margins in the coming years. However, I continued to reiterate that the stock was priced to perfection with little to no upside at that time.

Duolingo recently reported its Q4 FY24 earnings, where revenue and Adjusted EBITDA grew 39% and 49% YoY to $209.6M and $52.3M, respectively. During the quarter, the company continued to scale its user base, surpassing 40M DAUs (Daily Active Users), while the paid subscriber conversion rate rose on both a sequential and year-over-year basis to 8.8% as a result of the growing adoption of its genAI-powered Duolingo Max subscription tier.

However, the stock declined over 16% post-earnings. I believe that the weakness is attributed to two main factors. First, Duolingo guided for revenue in FY25 to grow at roughly 29.75%, in line with consensus estimates. For a stock that is priced to perfection, investors generally look for a sizable surprise in the forward guidance. Second, the management expects its Adjusted EBITDA margins to expand 180 basis points to 27.5% in FY25. However, this implies that the company is projected to grow its operating expenses at a faster rate than in FY24.

Moving forward, I believe volatility in Duolingo will continue, and the stock price will be under pressure, at least until the management demonstrates evidence of its revenues and profitability growing at a faster rate than it has projected for FY25. For now, I do not see a path for multiple expansion, as investor sentiment will likely be dampened in the short term. As a result, I am reducing my price target to $290 while maintaining my “hold” rating on the stock.

The good: Robust user growth and monetization as Duolingo Max sees growing adoption.

Duolingo reported its Q4 FY24 earnings recently, where revenue grew 39% YoY to $209.6M, beating estimates. Out of the $209.6M in revenue, Subscription revenue grew at a faster pace of 48% YoY to $174.3M, contributing over 83% of Total Revenue.

During the quarter, the company surpassed 40M in DAUs, which grew at 51% YoY, as a result of product improvements and their social-first creative marketing that has translated into superior user engagement and retention. Particularly, when it comes to marketing, the management noted that their Super Bowl ad, Duolingo on Ice, and “Owl Game” partnership with Netflix contributed to their user growth, while they simultaneously diversified their marketing channels to include YouTube Shorts and Instagram Reels, which has also seen terrific growth in views on a year-over-year basis.

Shifting gears to monetization, Duolingo ended the year with 9.5M paid subscribers, up 43% YoY. Meanwhile, their Paid Subscriber penetration as a percentage of MAUs (Monthly Active Users) also increased on a sequential and on a year-over-year basis to 8.8% as they made significant progress rolling their highest paid subscription tier, Duolingo Max, to a majority of their users. Particularly, Video Call, which is their GenAI-powered real-time conversation feature, has played a key role in driving a higher conversion rate of their Duolingo Max subscription tier, which now accounts for 5% of paid subscribers.

Looking forward into 2025, the management is looking to make their Video Call feature in Duolingo Max more robust by making Lily, their AI-powered conversation partner, more dynamic and interactive. I believe this will help Duolingo better differentiate its Duolingo Max subscription as a premium tier, thus leading to superior user monetization and conversion rates. At the same time, the management also committed to increasing their experiment velocity to make user experience more social and gamified through A/B testing while expanding their addressable market with the launch of new courses, such as Chinese for Korean speakers and German for Japanese speakers.

The bad: “Other” Revenue slows down, Tougher Comps ahead, while operating expenses are about to grow faster.

However, Duolingo’s “Other” Revenue (which consists of Advertising, Duolingo English Test and In-App Purchases) saw its growth rate decline 5% YoY to $33.5M, down from 10% in the previous quarter. While Subscription Revenue is doing most of the heavy lifting at the moment, a decline in the growth rate of “Other” Revenue could be pointing to a broader slowdown in macroeconomic trends.

At the same time, the company also saw its gross margins decrease by 120 basis points year-over-year to 71.9% from higher GenAI costs associated with the growing adoption of Duolingo Max as well as lower advertising revenue per DAU. For the full year FY24, the company saw its gross margins decline by 50 basis points to 72.8%.

Meanwhile, Duolingo generated $52.3M in Adjusted EBITDA, which grew at 49% YoY, with a margin expansion of 170 basis points to 25.0%. While the company continued to gain operating leverage across Research & Development, Sales & Marketing and General & Administrative expenses (as can be seen below) as a result of revenue growing faster than operating expenses and AI and automation tools helping them scale their content creation, the magnitude of margin expansion is smaller compared to previous quarters.

Moving forward, the management has guided for revenue in FY25 roughly 29.75% YoY to $970M, at a slower pace than the 41% YoY revenue growth in FY24. Furthermore, the management’s forward revenue guidance came at par with consensus estimates. For a stock that is priced to perfection, investors were likely expecting management to guide higher than what consensus estimates had projected for revenue growth in FY25.

However, I believe that the main reason behind the post-earnings decline in the stock price is associated with the company’s forward Adjusted EBITDA margin for FY25, which it estimates roughly at 27.5%. While it is 180 basis points higher than the company’s FY24 Adjusted EBITDA margin, the forward Adjusted EBITDA projection points to Duolingo growing its non-GAAP operating expenses at a faster rate than in FY24, assuming a gross margin of 73%. Let me explain.

In FY24, Duolingo grew its non-GAAP operating expenses at 19% to $354.8M. However, should Duolingo maintain its gross margins at 72.8-73% and grow its revenues at 29.75%, an Adjusted EBITDA margin of 27.5% will translate to $439M in non-GAAP operating expenses, representing a growth rate of 24%, higher than the previous year.

The way I see it, as Duolingo enters tougher comps, where revenue growth rate is expected to slow down, investors did not react positively to the company projecting to increase the pace of its operating expenses, leading to the stock losing over 16% of its share price on Friday, 28th February.

Revisiting my valuation

Looking forward, I will take the management’s FY25 revenue guidance and the consensus estimates for FY26 and FY27, where revenue is expected to slow down to the mid-twenties range, generating roughly $1.5B by FY27. This will be made possible by a combination of both Subscription and “Other” Revenue, as Duolingo continues to scale its user growth through its social-first marketing strategy while deepening adoption of its genAI-powered Duolingo Max subscription tier.

From a profitability standpoint, assuming that Duolingo is able to anchor its gross margins in the 73% range over the next three years, and non-GAAP operating expenses grow in the 20% range during this period of time, we should see its Adjusted EBITDA margin expand roughly 375 basis points to 31.25% by FY27, translating to an Adjusted EBITDA of $473M. This will be equivalent to a present value of $376M when discounted at 8%.

Assigning a price-to-earnings multiple of 34, which is roughly twice the average multiple of the S&P 500, given the projected growth rate of its earnings during this period of time, we arrive at a price target of $290, roughly 8% below the stock’s current levels.

My final verdict and conclusions

Although Duolingo is continuing to grow its user base and monetization through superior product innovation and engaging marketing experiences, I believe investor sentiment will remain dampened as the stock enters a period of tougher comps.

Furthermore, the management has guided its revenue to grow at par with consensus estimates, leaving little room for investors to get excited. At the same time, with its non-GAAP operating expenses projected to grow faster in FY25 (than in FY24), investors will remain cautious, at least until the management is able to demonstrate its forward revenues and profitability growing at a faster rate than it has currently projected.

As a result, I will remain on the sidelines while maintaining my “hold” rating with a price target of $290.

data.title: SCHD: The AI Frenzy Is Gone
data.description:
Investment thesis

My previous bullish thesis on the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (NYSEARCA:SCHD) aged decently with a 1.3% total return. However, the stock underperformed compared to the broader U.S. equity market since early October.

Despite this underperformance, I continue accumulating my position in SCHD as the latest earnings season revealed that the optimism around growth stocks is close to peak levels. President Trump's quite aggressive rhetoric on various issues is also not very good for long-term instruments like growth stocks. SCHD offers a perfect balance of dividend yield and growth, which is a good choice amid the current kind of fragile environment. The stock's dividend scorecard looks much better compared to peer large dividend ETFs. All in all, I reiterate "Strong buy" for SCHD.

Recent developments

Yesterday Nvidia (NVDA) released another impressive quarterly earnings with quite aggressive guidance. Nevertheless, there was no post-market spike as NVDA share price declined slightly. Overall, despite mostly positive earnings surprises, six out of the "Magnificent 7" stocks are currently cheaper than they were at the end of 2024.

I think that this is a clear sign for investors that the next few months might be quite bumpy for growth stocks. Such a cautious dynamic despite robust earnings suggests that optimism is at peak levels after massive rallies of 2023 and 2024. Google’s CEO stated in December 2024 that the ‘low-hanging fruit’ era for AI has passed—a view that now appears to have been accurate. This is a big reason why investors might consider rebalancing their portfolios by increasing the weight of safer assets.

It is also crucial to keep in mind that this year is also started with aggressive expansion of Chinese start-ups and large tech companies to the generative AI field. The DeepSeek R1 release shocked the American stock market, which challenged the AI leadership of the U.S. tech behemoths. There are also young and promising European players in the AI field like Nebius Group (NBIS), which also add competition to the global AI landscape.

Another big reason to be cautious on long-duration [i.e. growth stocks] stocks is the rapidly evolving geopolitical environment. Trump started his second presidential term quite aggressively and some of the initiatives are quite questionable. His trade war against China which was started during the first presidential term was more-or-less explainable by China's ambitious to become the world's economic and technological leader as well as the historical rivalry between the West and the East.

However, his second presidential term started with initiating trade war against America's historical allies and the largest trade partners like Mexico, Canada, and the European Union. I am not speaking about potential unfavorable geopolitical implications since I am not an expert in this area. What is more important in the context of the U.S. growth stocks is that increased tariffs on goods imported from the country's largest trade partners will extremely likely lead to a new spike in inflation.

Even without the effect of increased tariffs, the U.S. inflation is still higher than the Fed's target level of 2%. With inflation still running hot and vast uncertainty around Trump's next steps on tariffs, the Fed is extremely unlikely to rush with more interest rate cuts. This is another adverse short-term factor for growth stocks, meaning that valuation models will continue incorporating more aggressive discount rates.

The picture does not look beautiful with a tight monetary environment, elevated inflation expectations, and the AI frenzy gone. Thus, it is highly likely that investors will start to rebalance their portfolios by rotating from growth to value. SCHD appears to be one of the top safe haven options due to multiple reasons. The above pie chart shows us the sector-wise holdings breakdown. The top-3 sectors are all defensive, which is preferable in the current challenging environment for sectors like Technology or Communication.

In my previous SCHD thesis I explained in detail why I consider High Dividend Yield Index Fund ETF (VYM) and Dividend Appreciation Index Fund ETF (VIG) from Vanguard to be SCHD's main rivals. In terms of the by-sector breakdown these options look less attractive in the current unfavorable environment for growth stocks with their much less exposure to defensive sectors. The Technology sector is the number one holding for VIG and number two for VYM.

Moreover, SCHD offers a much better dividend scorecard compared to its closest rivals. The ETF is more attractive in terms of the yield and historical dividend growth. Given that frequency and consistency are almost identical for all three options, it is certain that SCHD is the highest-quality dividend machine among them.

Risks to my thesis

Trump is not only about the aggressive geopolitical rhetoric, but also about deregulation and plans to cut taxes. These are certainly positive factors for the U.S. business, and some of these steps might boost confidence in the America's stock market. Political decisions and announcements are not easily predicted, meaning that some sudden positive swings might happen as well. Moreover, decreased taxes and deregulation initiatives could mitigate the adverse effect on inflation from increased tariffs. As a result, my assertions about the more cautious Fed might not age well.

In addition to the inflation agenda, let's not forget about the importance of energy prices. The "Drill, baby, drill" agenda will boost oil and gas supply, which will weigh on global commodity prices. This is inherently disinflationary. Furthermore, we see that Trump's rhetoric on Russia is quite friendly, which might be another crucial catalyst that will weigh on energy prices. Russia is one of the largest oil and gas producers and exporters, meaning that stopping sanctions against Russian hydrocarbons will also boost global supply.

Last but not least, technological breakthroughs might also happen quite unexpectedly. The emergence of generative AI in early 2023 was sensational for the stock market, which has led to a big two-year rally for growth stocks. Tech giants invest in R&D and data centers more than ever, meaning that there is a probability of new groundbreaking technological releases.

Bottom line

To conclude, SCHD is still a "Strong buy". This looks like the best large dividend ETF option in the current environment as it offers strong exposure to defensive sectors and its dividend scorecard is by far the best compared to both VIG and VYM.

data.title: Broadcom: Margin Of Safety Still Low Despite Price Pullback
data.description:
AVGO stock: previous thesis and new developments

My last article on Broadcom Inc. (NASDAQ:AVGO) stock was published back in May 2024. That article was titled "Broadcom Stock: Time To Take Some Profit". In that article, I rated the stock as a hold based on a 2-stage discounted cash flow model. Based on the results, I concluded that:

Broadcom Inc. is currently priced for perfection, leading little margin of safety. For investors truly committed to the long term, there is plenty to like, such as its focus on enterprise infrastructure, strategic M&A, and AI potential. However, in the near term, the market's expectations for its EPS growth are overly optimistic in my view.

Since then, there have been many new catalysts evolving around AVGO stock. In the remainder of this article, I will focus on the top two on my list: the profit drivers updated in its recent FQ4 2024 earnings report (ER) and also its ongoing external acquisition plans. My thesis for this follow-up coverage is that AVGO still leaves a thin margin of safety under current conditions despite these new growth catalysts and the recent price pull back. Thus, this article reiterates my hold rating on the stock.

AVGO stock: new catalysts and earnings outlook

Let me start with the updates provided in its recent FQ4 2024 ER. AVGO released the ER on December 12, 2024. The updates were quite up beating, delivering a strong normalized EPS of $1.42, beating estimates by $0.03. GAAP EPS came in at $0.90, surpassing expectations even more by $0.20. Revenue reached $14.05B, missing estimates by a very slight margin of $6.74M. Looking ahead to the upcoming quarter (scheduled for March 6, 2025), AVGO is projected to deliver a normalized EPS of $1.51, translating into a sequential growth of more than 6%. Also note that its EPS revisions over the last 90 days show 21 upward adjustments and only 2 downward adjustments, indicating positive analyst sentiment.

There are indeed plenty of reasons to support the positive sentiment here. Like many of other prominent semiconductor companies, AVGO is also benefiting from artificial-intelligence (AI) and the AI-related expenditure from its customers. In addition to the AI-tailwind, AVGO is simultaneously integrating its massive acquisition of VMware, too. I see good odds for hyper-scaling on this front given this unique software business and thus have a favorable view on its long-term growth perspective.

AVGO stock: growth still fully priced in

Such a favorable view is in line with consensus projections for the next few years. As you can see from the following table, consensus EPS estimates point to a promising growth trajectory for AVGO in the next 5 years. For FY 2025, the consensus EPS is projected to be $6.35, representing a 30% YOY increase. The growth is expected to fluctuate somewhat, but is expected to stay in the double digits. All told, the average growth rate for the next 5 years is projected to be about 17% in CAGR terms.

However, the stock is also expensively priced. As seen in the chart above, the forward P/E is estimated at 31.15 based on FY 2025 EPS forecast. With an average growth rate of 17% as expected, the PEG (P/E growth) ratio is more than 1.8x, far above the golden standard of 1x most GARP (growth at reasonable price) investors seek.

Admittedly, the above accounting EPS overstates its valuation multiples. As a high-growth company, AVGO spends aggressively on growth CAPEX, while its maintenance CAPEX Is relatively low. As a reflection, the next chart compares AVGO Capital Expenditures and Total Depreciation and Amortization (D&A, which I use to approximate maintenance CAPEX here). As seen, its Capital Expenditures have been ramping up quickly in recent years. Starting below $3B in 2016, it doubled to over $6B by 2020, and more than tripled to the current level of $10 B. In contrast, its D&A remained relatively low and also stable around $548M.

To get a more quantitative estimate of its growth CAPEX expenditures, I followed Bruce Greenwald's method (as detailed in his book entitled Value Investing). Growth CAPEX should be considered part of the owner's earnings (OE) following the wisdom of Warren Buffet. When the growth CAPEX is added to the OE, my analyses based on the Greenwald method showed that AVGO's OE is on average ~23% higher than its accounting EPS in the past 3 years. Thus, its P/E based on OE would be about 23% lower, resulting in a PEG ratio of about 1.5x, still far about the 1x threshold.

Other risks and final thoughts

To keep my comments more balanced, it's worthwhile pointing out the limitations of my above analysis, which translates into additional upside risks for AVGO. First, my above analysis of its OE did not factor in its share repurchase programs. The chart shows AVGO's Net Common Buyback Yield in the past. As seen, the company has been consistently buying back its own shares since 2019, and the current buyback yield is 1.32%. Such consistent buybacks add a boost to its EPS growth, especially when the shares were bought back at a discount to fair value.

Second, my above OE analysis did not factor in external acquisitions either. AVGO has a successful record of making external acquisitions. There are a few promising candidates ongoing, with the acquisition of Intel's products division as a notable one as you can see from the news below:

Seeking Alpha News: Acquiring Intel's products division would be 'extremely lucrative' for Broadcom: analyst. If Broadcom (NASDAQ:AVGO) were to acquire Intel's (NASDAQ:INTC) products division, the potential deal would be "extremely lucrative" for the Hock Tan-led Broadcom, Piper Sandler said. "Assuming that AVGO management pays a premium to the current market cap and is able to turn this business around just as it has almost every other business that it has previously acquired, we think the numbers become extremely compelling," Piper Sandler analyst Harsh Kumar wrote in a note to clients.

All told, my overall impression is that the upside potential is in balance with the downside risks. I do see many growth catalysts from its latest ER (such as AI demand and VMware integration). But I think the elevated valuation, even after the price pull back in the past few days, already priced in these positives. As such, I don't see an obvious bias in its return/risk curve and maintain my hold rating.

data.title: Petrobras Q4 Earnings: Unpacking The Latest Concerns
data.description:
On February 20, I posted a Petrobras (PBR) earnings preview piece where I made it clear that the main topic of conversation on earnings day would be around the Brazil-based oil giant's ability to distribute robust dividends and especially extraordinary ones (i.e. beyond the regular ordinary dividends that Petrobras historically distributes).

As many investors seek to invest in Petrobras mostly because of its dividend appeal, I believed that much of the bullish or bearish outlook would be around this agenda, and so, with uncertain talk of extraordinary dividends, I decided at the time to adopt a position of staying on the sidelines, which turned out to be the right decision.

Here's exactly what I wrote:

“The main topic of conversation on earnings day, however, is likely to be around the distribution of dividends and the possibility of extraordinary dividends. The consensus for the quarter is for a dividend per share ranging from R$0.89 to R$1.48, or $0.15 to $0.25, which would yield a quarterly yield of between 2.3% and 3.9%.”

Assuming that many investors are drawn to the company primarily for its dividends, even if in the short term the distribution remains robust, in the long term, I see a deterioration in this pattern in the long term, which is bearish. This is especially concerning given that Brazil's equity risk premium stands at 7.6%. That is, with a dividend yield closer to this level, the risk return becomes less and less appealing.

If, on the one hand, Q4 earnings get a boost from extraordinary dividends, serving as a significant short-term catalyst for the company, on the other hand, I expect a proportionally bearish reaction if that doesn’t happen."

It turns out that I was wrongly optimistic in the short term awaiting an extraordinary dividend announcement, mainly because Brazil's federal government is facing a fiscal crisis and, as the company's main shareholder, would benefit from distributing dividends to strengthen its cash flow.

But it turns out that things ended up going in the opposite direction to my assumption, with Petrobras opting to step on the CapEx accelerator and push the focus on dividends even further to the sidelines.

Although the 4Q24 results are not necessarily a signal for panic (for current ADR holders that carry long-term profitable positions), the points of attention raised certainly cannot be ignored or softened with arguments that dividends and valuations remain attractive, in my opinion. It's true that in 4Q24 Petrobras' results were negatively impacted by exchange rate fluctuations, but there was an increase in leverage, weakening cash generation, uncertainties over CapEx anticipation, and lately, lower and shrinking dividends, the core of the company's investment thesis.

On the other hand, shares now trade at 19% down over the last twelve months, and 10% down over the last five trading days.

But also, even if earnings are declining (whether recurring or not), Petrobras today trades at an earnings yield of 19% versus 21% prior 4Q24 in January and much less attractive than the nearly 30% in April 2024. In other words, despite the falling share price, Petrobras isn't necessarily any cheaper now than it was in 3Q24.

For this reason, even if there are some possible catalysts for 1Q25 and full-year 2025, I am adopting an underperform stance for Petrobras shares post-4Q24, where the thesis seems to be stuck in terms of dividends growth and valuations that are not necessarily more attractive, which is ultimately what has mattered most to the majority of Petrobras shareholders.

Key Points of Concern Raised After 4Q Earnings

The headline on Petrobras' 4Q24 earnings came out like this on Bloomberg:

"Brazil's state-controlled oil company Petrobras plunged after it reported a surprise fourth-quarter loss and announced dividends that fell significantly short of expectations."

The bearish reaction in the share price immediately after the release of the results highlights the key issues investors were focused on. The market is digesting points of caution, even though that doesn't represent a drastic break from the excellent results that Petrobras has been delivering since 2017/2018—but it does mark a shift.

Starting with the fact that Petrobras reported a net loss to shareholders of—R$17 billion ($2.76 billion) and a net income of $7.5 billion in 2024 versus $24.9 billion in 2023. However, it is worth noting that the impact of the currency devaluation, which entered the financial result line, is only of an accounting nature related to the debts of Petrobras and its subsidiaries abroad. Excluding these effects, net income would result in R$17.8 billion ($3.09 billion) in 4Q24 and $19.4 billion in 2024.

The impact of the devaluation of the real against the dollar generated an accounting loss, since part of the company's debt is in dollars. Nevertheless, this impact is not necessarily bad for companies that trade commodities in dollars.

The weaker numbers than the market anticipated in terms of the bottom line came from EBITDA. This worsening is related to higher-than-expected costs, with EBITDA in the E&P line, Petrobras' main business focus, falling by 44.7% YoY.

Therefore, 4Q24 was a quarter marked by (1) tighter costs and expenses, (2) a more compressed Brent price, (3) lower E&P production, (4) refining margins still operating at levels of limited efficiency (8% in 4Q24 vs. historical 10%) and (5) structurally higher CAPEX, given the current mandate and more aggressive strategic plan with investments.

The key points to watch, however, are:

(1) Petrobras' indebtedness has increased, reaching the highest level of the last two years with a net debt/EBITDA LTM adjusted of 1.29x in 2024 vs. 0.85x in 2023 (see chart below);

(2) free cash flow of $23.3 billion in 2024 vs. $31 billion in 2023, an annual drop of 25% where part of this reduction is due to the increase in investments (CapEx) made by Petrobras and; (3) the same CapEx that Petrobras anticipated investments, especially in the Búzios basin. Although this is a very profitable reserve, the anticipation of these investments put pressure on cash flow in 4Q24 (down by 45.1% versus 4Q23) and may raise doubts about the profitability of these projects.

The last, but much less important, point that deserves special mention is the lower dividend—which is also a reflection of the higher indebtedness and the reduction in cash generation. It's worth noting that Petrobras' dividend policy is to distribute minimum mandatory dividends corresponding to 25% of net profit, and if gross debt is less than US$ 60 billion, the company can distribute 45% of its free cash flow.

Therefore, gross debt will end up at the limit in 2024 at $60.31 billion. In 2024 Petrobras distributed less dividends compared to last year, being $18.32 billion (versus $19.67 billion in 2023), approximately 78% of FCF being in 4Q24, an amount of $5.45 billion, without having had the distribution of extraordinary dividends in the quarter.

Why Things Are Looking Bad for Petrobras

Let's start with dividends.

In the last twelve months, Petrobras has had an incredible dividend yield of ~20%. For 2025, 2026 and 2027, the consensus implies a yield of 14%, 12.3% and 12.8%, respectively. Petrobras' CEO Magda Chambriard declared that it is "stepping on the accelerator" with the company investing in oil equipment and services, such as expanding a fleet of support vessels and expanding refining capacity.

Considering that investing in Brazil implies an equity risk premium of 7.6%, it's undeniable that the yield levels for PBR should remain at least robust—even with free cash flow being allocated to accelerating CapEx (investments) to increase production. But on the other hand, a decrease in dividend growth rates certainly doesn't sound bullish.

Additionally, regarding the investments, there are many inherent risks: (1) execution; (2) increased refining capacity, in a company that openly does not follow international parity; (3) a history of state corruption along these lines.

But going deeper into these investments, I want to take a closer look at the Equatorial Margin (which could even be a major risk to the bearish thesis).

This is Brazil's new oil frontier, stretching from the states of Rio Grande do Norte to Amapá, in the northeast and north of the country. Petrobras will invest $3 billion by 2029 to drill 15 wells, with a potential of 5-7.5 billion recoverable barrels, more than the pre-salt. At first, the estimated production in 2030 and beyond could sustain Petrobras for decades, at low cost ($20-25/barrel).

Petrobras is currently awaiting the environmental license from "Ibama" exploring the Foz do Amazonas in Q1 2025, and the Brazilian President Lula da Silva is pushing the environmental agency for supporting the exploration. It's worth noting that Ibama denied the license in 2023, facing resistance from indigenous communities and federal prosecutors.

In other words, Ibama's license in 1Q25 or 2Q25 could be a major catalyst for Petrobras shares, but this is highly speculative.

Another speculative point is that the U.S. dollar versus the Brazilian real dropped in 1Q25 and possibly, there will be no non-recurring events, so earnings should take a hit in 1Q (even without the cash effect). This could be a premise for an extraordinary dividend for 1Q25 or sometime in 2025. However, FX is another point that gets out of hand, and adopting a stance that the BRL will appreciate versus the USD is even more speculative, given all the fiscal crisis that Brazil has been facing throughout 2024 and is likely to face in 2025.

Finally, Petrobras has managed to readjust prices in line with the international price parity (IPP) of fuels.

In February, the average price charged by Petrobras refineries was R$3.06/liter for gasoline and R$3.84/liter for diesel. Thus, the prices charged by Petrobras refineries are practically in line with international parity, with a premium of 0.3% on gasoline (+R$0.01/liter) and in line with the price of diesel on the same basis.

This was great news for PBR investors since the discontinuation of this parity policy by Petrobras since 2023 requires a readjustment in fuel prices so that the company does not lose profitability. But it remains to be seen how consistent Petrobras will be in 2025 in keeping prices in line with international parity, without the Brazilian government interference, amid a scenario of high economic instability.

Final Remarks

I've been a harsh critic of Petrobras throughout my coverage of the company's ADR.

In large part, my skepticism rests on the premise that in order to continue generating alpha, Petrobras will need to maintain its appealing dividend thesis. In 2024, Petrobras delivered a total return to shareholders of ~20% considering the share price depreciation (-21%) and the payment of dividends (yielding ~20.8%).

The bar for shareholder returns has been set very high in the past couple of years, and it has left many investors overly demanding. This has also been enough to overshadow any concerns about the political risks tied to the company’s investment thesis.

Although the current yield and projections for the next two years are still arguably robust, the dividend distribution is a devolution versus previous years' levels, including 2024.

Additionally, as the company is in a stage of significant investments to expand production, free cash flow is likely to remain under pressure and persistent FX headwinds may put extra pressure on debt levels, and I fear that this may be enough to leave investors uneasy.

That said, I don't see the bearish reaction of the markets around PBR after 4Q24 as an exaggeration, but rather as the consolidation of a bearish pattern—something I wasn’t so sure about before 4Q24. For that reason, I'm updating my stance on PBR to underperform (which may sound contrarian for some). Even though Petrobras has one of the largest and highest-quality reserves in the world, at the end of the day, the company's strategy is too focused on shareholder returns—and the trend is leaning bearish.
